canto_title: The Furies of Fraud: The Biting Shadows and the Quarrel of the Counterfeiters

scenes.summary: Dante recounts the myth of Atamante, who went mad and killed his wife and children.
After the fall of Troy, Queen Hecuba reacts to the deaths of her children with animalistic despair.
Dante observes two nude, dead shadows biting each other like wild pigs.
The Aretin identifies one of the biting souls as Gianni Schicchi.
Dante urges the Aretin to identify the second shadow before it escapes.
The Aretin identifies the second soul as the wicked Mirra, who committed incest.
The Aretin explains that the other soul is Buoso Donati, a man who falsified his will.
As the biting shadows pass, Dante turns his gaze to other fraudulent souls.
Dante describes a man suffering from severe dropsy, with a distended belly and parched lips.
The man, Master Adam, laments his intense thirst and his previous earthly satisfaction.
Adam describes how the sight of the streams from the Casentino mountains exacerbates his thirst.
Adam reveals that his crime was the forgery of the Baptistery's official seal.
Adam declares he would trade his sight rather than see the souls of Guido or Alessandro.
Adam confirms the biting shadows are indeed inside the pit but laments his inability to move.
Adam explains how heavy his limbs are and how slowly he could move through the pit.
Adam admits he was driven to debase the florins to gain profit.
Dante asks Adam about the two steaming souls lying near his boundaries.
Adam identifies the souls as the woman who falsely accused Joseph and the Greek Sinon.
One of the souls strikes the other, producing a sound like a drum.
Adam strikes the soul back with his heavy arm, asserting his skill in striking.
The struck soul retorts that Adam was much faster when he was minting coins.
The dropsical man accuses Sinon of failing to be a truthful witness in Troy.
Sinon retorts that both he and Adam are guilty of falsehood.
The other soul reminds Sinon of his role in the deception of the Trojan Horse.
Sinon curses the man with thirst and the stagnant water in his belly.
Adam mocks the man's thirst by comparing his condition to the vanity of Narcissus.
Virgil interrupts Dante, warning him not to get too close to the angry souls.
Dante recoils in shame at his own reaction to the scene.
Dante describes his internal struggle and his desire to excuse himself from the sight.
Virgil advises Dante that true shame comes from greater sins and tells him to move on.
